title: The Largest Bookshelf Tour Ever Filmed: Inside a ClassicistÔÇÖs 20,000-Volume Library
description:
in Books, Education |   	June 1st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If you grew up in the last few gen┬şer┬şa┬ştions, chances are you did┬şnÔÇÖt get much of an e
body:
in Books, Education |   	June 1st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If you grew up in the last few gen┬şer┬şa┬ştions, chances are you did┬şnÔÇÖt get much of an edu┬şca┬ştion, if any, in Latin or ancient Greek. One long-made argu┬şment for phas┬şing them out of cur┬şric┬şu┬şla in Eng┬şlish-speak┬şing coun┬ştries holds that room must be made for Span┬şish, Man┬şdarin, and oth┬şer┬álan┬şguages actu┬şal┬şly used at scale in the mod┬şern world. Nowa┬şdays, when even those class┬şes face the pres┬şsure of extinc┬ştion,┬áadvo┬şca┬şcy for clas┬şsi┬şcal lan┬şguages exudes an ever stronger con┬ştrar┬şi┬şan appeal. ÔÇťDeadÔÇŁ though they may be, they also live on through not just the Romance lan┬şguages, but also the mighty hege┬şmon known as Eng┬şlish. Indeed, it makes sense to ask whether an Anglo┬şphone with┬şout knowl┬şedge of Latin or Greek tru┬şly under┬şstands his own native tongue.
Nor, accord┬şing to clas┬şsi┬şcist David But┬şter┬şfield, can one learn Latin with┬şout hav┬şing any Greek. Get┬şting a han┬şdle on both of those lan┬şguages and their sur┬şviv┬şing body of texts isnÔÇÖt just the work of a life┬ştime; it also fills a house, as evi┬şdenced by the two-and-a-half-hour video tour of But┬şter┬şfield┬şÔÇÖs per┬şson┬şal library above. (The sub┬şse┬şquent two hours con┬ştain But┬şter┬şfield┬şÔÇÖs intro┬şduc┬ştions to a selec┬ştion of par┬ştic┬şu┬şlar vol┬şumes from his many shelves.) Youtu┬şber Tim┬şo┬şthy Ken┬şny has pre┬şvi┬şous┬şly uploaded quite a few such videos on the col┬şlec┬ştions of seri┬şous bib┬şlio┬şphiles, but this one he describes as the largest ever attempt┬şed, includ┬şing the com┬şplete Loeb Clas┬şsi┬şcal Library, I Tat┬şti Renais┬şsance Library, and Pauly-Wis┬şsowa ency┬şclo┬şpe┬şdias.
Yet accord┬şing to But┬şter┬şfield him┬şself, a young man by the stan┬şdards of his pro┬şfes┬şsion and spe┬şcial┬şty, heÔÇÖs still got a lot of col┬şlect┬şing to do. HeÔÇÖs only about 80 per┬şcent of the way to a full set of Oxford Uni┬şver┬şsi┬şty PressÔÇÖ Very Short Intro┬şduc┬ştions, a series through which IÔÇÖve been grad┬şu┬şal┬şly mak┬şing my own way in recent years. Hav┬şing found that its books offer ÔÇťa real┬şly good view of what┬şev┬şer the top┬şic or per┬şson is,ÔÇŁ he decid┬şed to ÔÇťcol┬şlect all the vol┬şumes that inter┬şest┬şed me. And that emerged to be more than I thought, because I am inter┬şest┬şed in almost every┬şthing.ÔÇŁ But with all of us, no mat┬şter how broad┬şly curi┬şous, some of his inter┬şests are stronger than oth┬şers, as one might expect from a man with the patience to amass a great amount of man┬şu┬şals for writ┬şing Greek and Latin prose and verse made for school┬şboys (and, often, con┬ştain┬şing their doo┬şdles).
After spend┬şing a cou┬şple of decades at Cam┬şbridge, But┬şter┬şfield crossed the Atlantic to go from one of the old┬şest insti┬ştu┬ştions of high┬şer edu┬şca┬ştion to one of the very newest. HeÔÇÖs now Provost of and Pro┬şfes┬şsor of Latin at Ral┬şston Col┬şlege in Savan┬şnah, Geor┬şgia, which received its first cohort of stu┬şdents in 2022. With its mas┬şterÔÇÖs degree pro┬şgram close┬şly focused on ancient, medieval and mod┬şern lit┬şer┬şa┬şture and art con┬şsid┬şered foun┬şda┬ştion┬şal to West┬şern civ┬şi┬şliza┬ştion, it seems like the kind of insti┬ştu┬ştion designed to attract some┬şone like But┬şter┬şfield, who was already win┬şning prizes for his library in or short┬şly after his col┬şlege days. ÔÇťI canÔÇÖt see myself relax┬şing until I have accu┬şmu┬şlat┬şed around 10,000 books,ÔÇŁ he said in a 2008 inter┬şview. His home, as cap┬ştured in Ken┬şnyÔÇÖs video, now con┬ştains dou┬şble that amount, but the┬áthu┬şmos clear┬şly has┬şnÔÇÖt desert┬şed him just yet.
Relat┬şed con┬ştent:
Watch Umber┬şto Eco Walk Through His Immense Pri┬şvate Library: It Goes On, and On, and On!
Jorge Luis Borges Selects 74 Books for Your Per┬şson┬şal Library
Take a Vir┬ştu┬şal Tour of Jane AustenÔÇÖs Library
Dis┬şcov┬şer the 1126 Books in John CageÔÇÖs Per┬şson┬şal Library: Fou┬şcault, Joyce, Wittgen┬şstein, Vir┬şginia Woolf, Buck┬şmin┬şster Fuller & More
The 321 Books in David Fos┬şter WallaceÔÇÖs Per┬şson┬şal Library: From Blood Merid┬şi┬şan to Con┬şfes┬şsions of an Unlike┬şly Body┬şbuilder
Why Learn Latin?: 5 Videos Make a Com┬şpelling Case That the ÔÇťDead Lan┬şguageÔÇŁ Is an ÔÇťEter┬şnal Lan┬şguageÔÇŁ
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Hip 1960s Latin Teacher Translated Beatles Songs into Latin for His Students: Read Lyrics for ÔÇťO Teneum Manum,ÔÇŁ ÔÇťDiei Duri NoxÔÇŁ & More
description:
in Language, Music |   	June 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
IÔÇÖve inter┬şact┬şed with many┬áenter┬ştain┬şing lan┬şguage-learn┬şing resources in var┬şi┬şous
body:
in Language, Music |   	June 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
IÔÇÖve inter┬şact┬şed with many┬áenter┬ştain┬şing lan┬şguage-learn┬şing resources in var┬şi┬şous classesÔÇöfrom minis┬şeries in Span┬şish to com┬şic books in FrenchÔÇöall geared toward mak┬şing the unfa┬şmil┬şiar lan┬şguage rel┬şe┬şvant to dai┬şly life. Learn┬şing coun┬şter┬şin┬ştu┬şitive pro┬şnun┬şci┬şa┬ştions, pars┬şing a new sys┬ştem of gram┬şmar, or mem┬şo┬şriz┬şing the gen┬şders of word after word can be labo┬şri┬şous and intim┬şi┬şdat┬şing in the class┬şroom. Doing so in every┬şday pop cul┬ştur┬şal set┬ştings, not as much.
When it comes to the teach┬şing of dead lan┬şguages, the resources can seem less approach┬şable. I cer┬ştain┬şly appre┬şci┬şate the lit┬şer┬şary and rhetor┬şi┬şcal genius of Vir┬şgil, Ovid, Horace, Cicero, and Julius Cae┬şsar. But dur┬şing my high school years, I did not always find their work easy to read in Eng┬şlish, much less in for┬şmal clas┬şsi┬şcal Latin. The ela┬ştion I felt after suc┬şcess┬şful┬şly trans┬şlat┬şing a pas┬şsage was some┬ştimes damp┬şened as I puz┬şzled over his┬ştor┬şi┬şcal notes and gloss┬şes that often┬áleft me with┬ámore ques┬ştions than answers.

ThatÔÇÖs not at all to say that stu┬şdents of Latin shouldnÔÇÖt be exposed to cul┬ştur┬şal and his┬ştor┬şi┬şcal con┬ştext or read the finest exem┬şplars of the writ┬şten lan┬şguage. Only that a break from the heavy stuff now and then goes a long way. Might I sub┬şmit to Latin instruc┬ştors one inge┬şnious tool from Eddie OÔÇÖHara, for┬şmer British Labour Par┬şty MP and clas┬şsics teacher? OÔÇÖHara passed away in May 2016, and not long after his death, his son Ter┬şry OÔÇÖHara tweet┬şed┬áthese trans┬şla┬ştions of Bea┬şt┬şles songs┬á(includ┬şing two Christ┬şmas tunes) his father made in the 60s for his stu┬şdents. At the time, these were the height of pop cul┬şture rel┬şe┬şvance, and, while a far cry from the com┬şplex┬şi┬şties of the Aeneid, a fun way for Latin learn┬şers to relate to a lan┬şguage that can seem cold and impos┬şing.
I will admit, my Latin has fall┬şen into such a state┬áthat I canÔÇÖt imme┬şdi┬şate┬şly vouch for the accu┬şra┬şcy or ele┬şgance of these trans┬şla┬ştions (ÔÇťcue fierce argu┬şments among Latin gram┬şmar┬şi┬şans,ÔÇŁ replies one Twit┬şter user), but thereÔÇÖs no rea┬şson to┬ádoubt Mr. OÔÇÖHara knew his stuff. ÔÇťÔÇťHe was a born edu┬şca┬ştor,ÔÇŁ his son remem┬şbers, ÔÇťHe was a teacher and clas┬şsi┬şcist by back┬şground and he had a strong inter┬şest in edu┬şca┬ştion┬şal mat┬şters and Greek cul┬ştur┬şal her┬şitage.ÔÇŁ Edu┬şcat┬şed him┬şself at Mag┬şdalen Col┬şlege, Oxford, OÔÇÖHara taught at Perse School, Cam┬şbridge, Birken┬şhead School, and in the ear┬şly 70s, C.F. Mott Col┬şlege in the Bea┬şt┬şlesÔÇÖ own Liv┬şer┬şpool.

In addi┬ştion to his role as a states┬şman, the Liv┬şer┬şpool Echo remem┬şbers┬áOÔÇÖHaraÔÇÖs many decades as┬áÔÇťa pop┬şu┬şlar teacher who brought class┬şes to life trans┬şlat┬şing Bea┬şt┬şles lyrics into Latin.ÔÇŁ We do not have any indi┬şca┬ştion of whether he actu┬şal┬şly tried to sing the lyrics, though his stu┬şdents sure┬şly must have attempt┬şed it. What must the cho┬şrus of ÔÇťAll My Lov┬şingÔÇŁ sound like as ÔÇťIta totum amorem dabo, Tibi totum, numquam cess┬şa┬şbaÔÇŁ? Or ÔÇťShe Loves YouÔÇŁ as ÔÇťAmat te, mehercleÔÇŁ? Singing them to myself, I can see that OÔÇÖHara was sen┬şsi┬ştive to the meter of the orig┬şi┬şnal Eng┬şlish in his Latin ren┬şder┬şings. But IÔÇÖd real┬şly love to see some┬şone set these to music and make a video. Any of our read┬şers up to the chal┬şlenge?
Final┬şly, since ear┬şly six┬şties Bea┬şt┬şles lyrics arenÔÇÖt as like┬şly to engage stu┬şdents in 2017, what pop cul┬ştur┬şal mate┬şr┬şi┬şal would you trans┬şlate todayÔÇöclassics teach┬şers out thereÔÇöto reach the bemused, bewil┬şdered, and the bored? If youÔÇÖre already hard at work using hip resources in the class┬şroom, please do share them with us in the com┬şments!
Note: To view the images in a larg┬şer for┬şmat, please click on the links to these indi┬şvid┬şual images: Image 1 - Image 2 ÔÇö Image 3. When the image opens, click on it again to zoom in.
Note 2: An ear┬şli┬şer ver┬şsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
Hear NirvanaÔÇÖs ÔÇťSmells Like Teen Spir┬şitÔÇŁ Per┬şformed in Clas┬şsi┬şcal Latin
Can Mod┬şern-Day Ital┬şians Under┬şstand Latin? A YouTu┬şber Puts It to the Test on the Streets of Rome
They Might Be GiantsÔÇÖ John Lin┬şnell Releas┬şes an EP of Songs in Latin
The Sto┬şry of┬áLorem Ipsum: How Scram┬şbled Text by Cicero Became Used by Type┬şset┬şters Every┬şwhere
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬şcian based in Durham, NC.┬á

title: Pacific Surf Takes Center Stage in Craig Hubbard's Dreamy Photos
description: When Craig Hubbard moved from Brooklyn to Venice Beach in 2013, he had an established creative career as an animator and comic book colorist, but
body:
When Craig Hubbard moved from Brooklyn to Venice Beach in 2013, he had an established creative career as an animator and comic book colorist, but it had been a long time since he had picked up a camera. The golden hour hues of the West CoastÔÇÖs legendary sunsets reacquainted him with lens-based work, and he began documenting the areas he frequented in his spare time. ÔÇťAs an avid surfer and former skater,┬áI gravitate toward skateparks and water,ÔÇŁ he tells Colossal. And with the ocean, of course, come the waves.
Venice Beach is a funky, coastal Los Angeles neighborhood that has retained its laid-back, surf-loving vibe despite new developments. Surfers await swells in areas like the Breakwater and the Venice Beach Pier, and Hubbard heads out with his own board and his camera. ÔÇťDusk, dawn, and fog banks activate my senses,ÔÇŁ he says. Tapping into his enthusiasm for graphic design, he focuses on bold outlines and forms, high contrast, and the energy of directionality and motion. ÔÇťNature ultimately does the heavy lifting, though,ÔÇŁ he adds.

HubbardÔÇÖs photos are ethereal and cinematic, with surfers and wave crests illuminated by the early morning sun or backdropped by the marine layer. Sometimes the intense spray, curl, shoulder, or lip become the sole subjects of the portraits. ÔÇťThe water is the muse and artist,ÔÇŁ Hubbard recently told an interviewer. ÔÇťIÔÇÖm just a biased translator and documentarian. Lastly, my ego relaxes in the ocean; the need to peacock recedes. This is where my best work comes fromÔÇöor favorite, I should say.ÔÇŁ 
Follow HubbardÔÇÖs work on Instagram and see some of his videos on YouTube.

schema.headline: Pacific Surf Takes Center Stage in Craig Hubbard's Dreamy Photos

title: An Interactive Sculpture by Wade and Leta Celebrates the Sun-Bleached Australian Landscape
description: In SydneyÔÇÖs Circular Quay, a 6.5-meter-tall installation spins, twirls, and totters amid a public thoroughfare. Titled ÔÇťThere, Now, Here,ÔÇŁ the kin
body:
In SydneyÔÇÖs Circular Quay, a 6.5-meter-tall installation spins, twirls, and totters amid a public thoroughfare. Titled ÔÇťThere, Now, Here,ÔÇŁ the kinetic artwork is by the Brooklyn-based duo Wade and Leta and is in almost constant motion, thanks to wind, motors, and willing participants hopping on a see-saw.
With black and white stripes alongside a more muted palette, the colors of the playground-style project reference Dorothea MackellarÔÇÖs beloved poem ÔÇťMy Country,ÔÇŁ which professes her devotion to the Australian landscape and what she dubs the ÔÇťsunburnt country.ÔÇŁ Harnessing the washed-out tones of a sun-bleached environment, the artists present their signature bold works in more subtle hues, as if the pieces have been baking under the light for years.

A sonic component created by Josh Burgess accompanies the sculpture and can be manipulated by the public through accessible controls. ÔÇťIf one were to listen closely, they can hear the rush of water on the rocks, the dings of the light rail, the crosswalk signal, and most importantly, the local wildlife,ÔÇŁ the artists say. ÔÇťOur favorite piece is a nod to the ÔÇśbush doofÔÇÖ using the sounds of a lyrebird as the structure.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťThere, Now, HereÔÇŁ is the pairÔÇÖs first public work in Australia and part of the annual light and music festival Vivid Sydney. Find more on Wade and LetaÔÇÖs Instagram.

schema.headline: An Interactive Sculpture by Wade and Leta Celebrates the Sun-Bleached Australian Landscape

title: 'Keith Haring in 3D' Highlights the Artist's Prolifically Art-Filled Life
description: New York City in the 1980s felt like a very different place. Imagine subway cars cloaked inside-out in graffiti and Times Square without the monum
body:
New York City in the 1980s felt like a very different place. Imagine subway cars cloaked inside-out in graffiti and Times Square without the monumental LED screens. Evidenced by the likes of photographers Steven Siegel, Willy Spiller, and Jamel Shabazz, not to mention Charlie AhearnÔÇÖs Wild Style (1982), a period of intense, new, rough-around-the-edges energy was canonized. The era marked the birth of hip hop and New Wave, MTV, iconic fashion, legendary nightlife, and Pop Art.
In 1978, just prior to the economy reeling during a major recession, a 20-year-old Keith Haring (1958-1990) moved to Manhattan to study at the School of Visual Arts. ÔÇťI arrived in New York at a time when the most beautiful paintings being shown in the city were on wheelsÔÇöon trainsÔÇöpaintings that traveled to you instead of vice versa,ÔÇŁ he said in a piece writing published by the The Keith Haring Foundation. The artist was fascinated by peopleÔÇÖs responses to art encountered out in the open and unexpectedlyÔÇöwhen it found its way into daily life and became a conduit to conversation and curiosity.
Untitled (1983), Sumi ink on paper screen, 36 x 65 inches. Collection of KAWS, ┬ę Keith Haring Foundation, courtesy of the Foundation and Gladstone. Photo by David Regen
Whether with chalk or black paint, Haring could create decisive, confident line drawings of angels, UFOs, dancing figures, snakes, and other motifs virtually anywhere, many of which were temporary. His work is a highlight of the rescued Luna Luna amusement park, and a mural in Amsterdam was obscured by cladding for three decades before being rediscovered. My dad fondly recalls seeing HaringÔÇÖs paintings in the hallway of the former Manhattan Pearl Paint art supplies store in 1980. I grew up recognizing his signature cartoonish style long before I knew who he was, wearing his work on a favorite T-shirt. And itÔÇÖs this prescient ÔÇťart everywhereÔÇŁ focus that grounds an exhibition opening this week at Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art called Keith Haring in 3D.
While he didnÔÇÖt consider himself a graffiti artist, Haring reveled in the technical precision of tags and unique interventions by street artists like Fab 5 Freddy, Lady Pink, Jean-Michel Basquiat as SAMO with collaborator Al Diaz, and many others were painting all over the city. ÔÇťGraffiti spoke of a world that was hip and streetwise, creative and spontaneous and undergroundÔÇöall that he admired and wanted to be,ÔÇŁ says the foundation. And as the trains rolled through subterranean stations lined with advertisements, Haring noticed something else: ready-made blank canvases.
During the recession, advertisers pulled their investment in subway station ad space, and the MTA replaced empty billboards with large sheets of black paper. By this time, Haring was already interested in the idea of art outside of gallery and museum spaces and how ÔÇťdifferent people saw different things in the drawings,ÔÇŁ he says. As he made big works in an open-air space, he was fascinated by the number of people who would stop and the conversations he work would ignite. ÔÇťThis was the first time I realized how many people could enjoy art if they were given the chance,ÔÇŁ he said.
HaringÔÇÖs subway series, Art in Transit, launched him to the apex of the 1980s art scene, where Andy Warhol was already cementing Pop ArtÔÇÖs presence and a circle of graffiti artists, performers, and other creatives were defining the look, sounds, and feel of the decade. Haring made his drawings very quickly to avoid arrestÔÇöthe police hauled him away on at least one occasionÔÇöand his friend, photographer Ivan Dalla Tana, documented many of the works before they were torn down or destroyed. Fortunately, a handful survive, including one in Keith Haring in 3D.
Installation view of ÔÇśKeith Haring in 3DÔÇÖ
The tall chalk drawing on black paper is one of few two-dimensional works in the show, but itÔÇÖs one of many in the collection of Larry Warsh, who has collaborated with curator Glenn Adamson to bring together a wide range of HaringÔÇÖs sculptural and multimedia pieces. Today, HaringÔÇÖs work is among some of the most recognizable by mainstream audiences, yet despite critiques that his work has become ÔÇťsanitizedÔÇŁ in its commercializationÔÇösomething he was actively, and even controversially, a proponent of during his lifetimeÔÇöviewers are invited into a unique dialogue with literally a new dimension to his work.
The exhibition is situated within a long, open space, so that viewers can see from one end to the other and meander through different areas free from a prescribed or chronological route. Most of the peripheral wall space is also entirely empty, steering visitors into the center to circumambulate a wide variety of forms and installations. Wandering around steel sculptures, ceramic vessels, archival items, and paintings on numerous found objects, the ÔÇťall-overÔÇŁ sense of HaringÔÇÖs oeuvre is manifest. I get the sense that Haring could see the potential in any object or space. If something had a surface, it could be art. How or where you personally encounter it, however, is fundamentally a part of the experience, and this is woven into the exhibitionÔÇÖs design.
From inflatable versions of his iconic ÔÇťRadiant BabyÔÇŁ motif to an altarpiece made following his diagnosis with AIDS to a series of giant, router-carved ÔÇťtotems,ÔÇŁ the works in Keith Haring in 3D celebrate experimentation and collaboration. The exhibition also spotlights, if incidentally, imperative issues in contemporary art today, from cultural appropriation to queer experience, not to mention his candid and direct approach to sharing his experiences with AIDS, from which he died at the age of 31.
Many of the artworks in the exhibition are drawn from WarshÔÇÖs personal collection. He had the foresight to collect artworks and fragments of HaringÔÇÖs studio along with hand-painted garments, the embellished hoods of damaged yellow New York City taxi cabs, a headboard, and even a refrigerator tagged by an array of graffiti artists. With a magpie-like eye for the artistÔÇÖs recognizably bold-lined paintings, Warsh rescued an illustrated steel I-beam from the building Haring worked in on Broome Street, plus jackets and other garments that the artist painted, among many other objects. 
Untitled (1986), acrylic paint on television, 20 x 28 x 14 inches. Courtesy of Larry Warsh, ┬ę Keith Haring Foundation
Warsh has long been fascinated by the way the artist applied his visual language to just about anything he could get his hands on. A papier-m├óch├ę sculpture called ÔÇťUntitled (Elephant)ÔÇŁ has a unique story to it, too. Adamson shares that the elephant belonged to Warhol, who encouraged Haring to add his own interpretation, but hidden beneath its black-and-white composition is actually an original pink-toenailed version by Basquiat.
A series of works Haring called Totems were inspired by Native American totem poles of the Pacific Northwest region, which he viewed as symbols of community and unity. Large wall-hung mask works are clearly influenced by African masking traditions, coated in HaringÔÇÖs characteristic lines and bold shapes. Adamson acknowledges that today, we view a white artistÔÇÖs appropriation of these cultural customs through a different lens, and he expounds on HaringÔÇÖs interpretation of the ÔÇťtotemicÔÇŁ in a recent article published in Artforum.
Other facets of the exhibition highlight the role of music and pop culture, the New York City club scene, the commodification of art, and HaringÔÇÖs death from AIDS. A number of posters and merchandise-type objects nod to the artistÔÇÖs Pop Shop, a retail-meets-art-installation he opened in 1986 in New York CityÔÇÖs Soho neighborhood. It may be seen in the spirit of Claes OldenburgÔÇÖs The Store installation in 1961, which also circumvented the conventional gallery show with a DIY, entrepreneurial spiritÔÇösomething we see so much of today with the aid of social media but at the time was virtually unheard of. HaringÔÇÖs Pop Shop was controversial, but it was meant to prove a point: ÔÇťItÔÇÖs about participation on a big level,ÔÇŁ he said. He wanted his art to be as accessible to as many people as possible.
That HaringÔÇÖs work was virtually everywhereÔÇömusic videos featuring Madonna and Grace Jones, on advertisements, in fashion, throughout subway stationsÔÇöis the guiding principle behind Keith Haring in 3D also marking the inaugural show in Crystal BridgesÔÇÖ expansion. The entire permanent collection has been re-imagined throughout a series of both existing and new spaces, which will open in their entirety this weekend.
Adamson and Warsh originally conceived of Keith Haring in 3D as strictly a book project, but it quickly evolved into something much more. A new book of the same title does coincide with the show, positioning the artistÔÇÖs three-dimensional works in a new light. Find your copy on Bookshop, and visit the exhibition in Bentonville, Arkansas, starting June 6 and continuing through January 25, 2027. You might also enjoy the Keith Haring Pop Up Book by Poposition Press.
Installation view of ÔÇśKeith Haring in 3D.ÔÇÖ Photo by Kate Mothes
Untitled (1983), Sumi ink and acrylic on found hide, 38 x 40 inches. Courtesy of Jeffrey D. Chaddock & Mark A. Marrow, ┬ę Keith Haring Foundation
Installation view of ÔÇśKeith Haring in 3D.ÔÇÖ Photo by Kate Mothes
Installation view of ÔÇśKeith Haring in 3D.ÔÇÖ Photo by Kate Mothes
Untitled (1986), enamel on 1963 Buick Special, 189 x 71 x 54 inches. Courtesy of Larry Warsh, ┬ę Keith Haring Foundation

schema.headline: 'Keith Haring in 3D' Highlights the Artist's Prolifically Art-Filled Life

title: Hilde Lynn Helphenstein of ÔÇťJerry GogosianÔÇŁ Found Dead in Brazil
description:
News
                The curator and commentator built a following for her online persona and satirical memes of the art world.
body:
News
                The curator and commentator built a following for her online persona and satirical memes of the art world.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Hilde Lynn Helphenstein at the Wallace Collection Young Benefactors' Gala on June 24, 2024 in London (photo Dave Benett/Getty Images)
    
        
            Hilde Lynn Helphenstein, the curator and art-world satirist widely known by the moniker Jerry Gogosian, has died at age 40. Police in Brazil have opened a ÔÇťsuspicious deathÔÇŁ investigation after Helphenstein was found dead in a S├úo Paulo hotel room on Sunday, May 31, as first reported by the local outlet Globo.In a statement to Hyperallergic, a spokesperson for Rosewood S├úo Paulo, a luxury hotel in the city's Bela Vista district, confirmed that Helphenstein was found deceased in one of its rooms on Sunday afternoon."Since the incident, the hotel has provided full collaboration with the competent authorities, promptly providing all the information requested to assist in the investigation," the spokesperson said, noting that the hotel would not comment further out of "respect for the privacy of the guest, their family members, and the work of the responsible authorities.ÔÇŁA spokesperson for the city of S├úo Paulo told Hyperallergic that police were investigating the death of a 40-year-old woman, but did not name Helphenstein or provide the cause of death in their statement.┬áHelphenstein maintained a following of nearly 150,000 on her popular Instagram account, where she began posting memes about the art world under the initially anonymous parody persona Jerry Gogosian in 2018. Seven years later, she announced that she was moving away from the satirical project. ÔÇťI have so loved and enjoyed being Jerry, but it is time to let it go,ÔÇŁ Helphenstein wrote in 2018.Her short-form videos and posts that cut through the blue-chip market's self-seriousness allowed her to expand her online footprint into a larger brand. In an interview with W magazine, Helphenstein said she aspired to be ÔÇťthe female Anthony Bourdain of the art world.ÔÇŁIn 2022, she curated an online show of works by emerging artists at Sotheby's titled┬áSuggested Followers: How the Algorithm is Always Right. Helphenstein also hosted a contemporary art podcast, "Art Smack," on Spotify, and moved the show to the creator subscription site Patreon last year. On various platforms, including a Substack blog, Helphenstein publicly discussed art-sector politics, industry friendships, and her personal struggles with depression.┬áAccording to her website, she was also pursuing an MBA at New York University.Hyperallergic has reached out to a press contact for Helphenstein for comment.
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Obituary

schema.headline: Hilde Lynn Helphenstein of ÔÇťJerry GogosianÔÇŁ Found Dead in Brazil

title: Unofficial Iraqi Pavilion Pops Up in Venice, but Not That One
description:
Feature
                Ali Eyal and David Horvitz satirize AmericaÔÇÖs oil war in an installation at a Chevron gas station on Ven
body:
Feature
                Ali Eyal and David Horvitz satirize AmericaÔÇÖs oil war in an installation at a Chevron gas station on Venice Boulevard. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Artist Ali Eyal with his unsanctioned Iraqi "pavilion," titled "Welcome to Iraq." (all photos Matt Stromberg/Hyperallergic)
    
        
            VENICE, CALIFORNIA ÔÇö On a bustling corner along Venice Boulevard about a mile and a half from the beach, artist Ali Eyal stood outside a Chevron gas station last Friday, May 29, posing as a black-market petrol salesman. On the screen of an old-fashioned television set, he had painted the words ÔÇťWe have oil hereÔÇŁ in ornate Arabic calligraphy. Atop the set sat four jugs resembling those filled by roadside gas vendors, their exteriors painted a noxious shade of ochre. The level of ÔÇťgasÔÇŁ is consecutively diminished in each one, until it transforms into a thin red sunset. ÔÇťIÔÇÖm ÔÇśduskaphobic,ÔÇÖÔÇŁ Eyal told Hyperallergic. ÔÇťBad things always happened after dark.ÔÇŁThe one-day pop-up installation was EyalÔÇÖs unofficial Iraqi Pavilion for the 61st Venice Biennale, curated by fellow artist David Horvitz, who mocked up a faux press release complete with a fuzzy Biennale logo, consistent with his persistent form of art trickery. ÔÇťIf Venice doesnÔÇÖt officially recognize a pavilion, can we fill that gap,ÔÇŁ he quipped. Iraq has not had an official pavilion at the contemporary arts festival since 2019, when the Ruya Foundation presented a solo exhibition by Serwan Baran, whose expressionistic paintings confront the horrors of war.Fellow artist David Horvitz curated the one-day installation at a Chevron gas station.Titled ÔÇťWelcome to Iraq,ÔÇŁ EyalÔÇÖs temporary artwork shared a name with the official Iraqi pavilion from 2013, which showcased a diverse range of work by 11 artists.┬áNow based in Los Angeles, Eyal grew up in Baghdad during the United States occupation of Iraq, and his large-scale, dream-like paintings conjure his childhood memories, both traumatic and hopeful. Growing up under sanctions, fuel was scarce, often obtainable only through illicit, informal networks. At his unsanctioned pavilion last week, Eyal was not actually selling gas, but rather pocket-sized oil pastel drawings of candles in bottles, his familyÔÇÖs only source of light when the power failed.┬áÔÇťThey said they would bomb us into the stone age, and they did,ÔÇŁ he recalled. Although rooted in the aftermath of the 2003 invasion of Iraq, the themes addressed in EyalÔÇÖs pavilion have a tragic resonance with the US and IsraelÔÇÖs attacks on Iran and the demand for oil and gas underlying both conflicts. The war has resulted in deadly fuel shortages around the world, notably leaving Cuba, where US sanctions have exacerbated the energy crisis, in the dark.A haphazardly written sign announcing the installation. "We accept cash or Venmo," it says.As with all his work, Eyal presented a particular vision of national identity, filtered through individual experience. The project straddled different methods of cultural and economic distribution, from the discrete nationalistic displays of the Biennial to the global infrastructure of resource extraction and exchange. At a mundane Venice intersection, his unsanctioned Venetian outpost provided a darkly comic rejoinder to both.┬áAs Eyal hawked his drawings to passersby, he was met with a mixture of indifference and curiosity. ÔÇťWeÔÇÖre from Ireland,ÔÇŁ said one couple, rebuffing HorvitzÔÇÖs attempts to hand them a flyer.┬áWhile he was setting up, Chevron employees asked him to relocate to the edge of the property, which serendipitously placed the installation directly beneath a sign announcing gasoline prices between six and seven dollars per gallon. The juxtaposition rendered EyalÔÇÖs bootleg stand all the more prescient.ÔÇťOil changed my life as an Iraqi forever,ÔÇŁ he said. ÔÇťThis is my own Iraq.ÔÇŁ
                  
                      
                        Feature
                      
                      
                        Los Angeles
                      
                      
                        California
                      
                      
                        Ali Eyal
                      
                      
                        Venice Biennale

schema.headline: Unofficial Iraqi Pavilion Pops Up in Venice, but Not That One
